channel.title: Posts on Yuliia Kostrikova
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Yuliia Kostrikova

item.title: the dangerous detour
item.description: Why agentic AI has no place running your IaC — and what 9 years with Terraform taught me about professional responsibility.

item.title: the dangerous detour
item.description: Why agentic AI has no place running your IaC — and what 9 years with Terraform taught me about professional responsibility.

item.title: HowTo: Fix iSCSI mounts after reboot
item.description: a follow-up to my previous article on PXE/iSCSI setup, addressing the annoying issue of iSCSI auto-mounting failure

item.title: k8s cluster on Raspberry PI's with iSCSI block storage
item.description: exploring the challenges of running a k8s cluster on Raspberry PI's with PXE network boot and overcoming containerd storage limitations using iSCSI

item.title: Detailed HowTo: PXE boot multiple RPi (Birthday Edition 🍰)
item.description: PXE (Preboot eXecution Environment) is a client-server environment which makes possible to boot and install operating systems without the need of physical media.

item.title: Setting Up a DHCP Server on Synology NAS in a UniFi Dream Machine SE Subnet
item.description:
In this blog post, I’ll guide you through the process of setting up a DHCP server on your Synology NAS within a subnet created on a UniFi Dream Machine Special Edition (UDM SE).

Prerequisites

Before we start, ensure you have the following:

  1. UDM SE: UniFi Dream Machine Special Edition.
  2. Synology NAS: Network Attached Storage device from Synology.
  3. Raspberry Pi: Any model will suffice.
  4. Ethernet cables: To connect your devices.

Step-by-Step Guide

1. Create a Subnet on UDM SE

  1. Log in to your UniFi Router: Access your UDM SE via the UniFi Network application.

item.title: RPI 5 on Ubuntu 24.04 (Headless): Connect to WiFi / Mobile Hotspot
item.description:
RPI 5 on Ubuntu 24.04 (headless): Connect to WiFi / Mobile Hotspot

Introduction

In my free time, I enjoy tinkering with my home lab, exploring new projects and challenges. Recently, I decided to connect my Raspberry Pi 5 to a mobile hotspot—a seemingly straightforward task. However, it turned out not so smooth. In this post, I’ll walk you through how I resolved the error brcmfmac: brcmf_set_channel: set chanspec fail, reason -52 and successfully connected Raspberry Pi to a mobile hotspot.

item.title: Automating UML Diagrams with ChatGPT and PlantUML
item.description: Learn how to effortlessly visualize system designs, saving time on syntax and focusing on architecting solutions

item.title: SAA-C03:: Introduction to Cloud Computing and AWS
How to Mount Synology NAS to RaspberryPi via NFS
item.description:
My Use case

To establish a seamless synchronization of project files between a MacBook (working station) and a Raspberry Pi (hosting server), allowing for automatic updates and accessibility of the project from both devices.

device/tool version
DS420+ DSM 7.2-64570
Raspberry PI 400

The user begins working on the project using the IDE on the MacBook, editing and updating project files within the synchronized folder. As changes are made, they are automatically reflected in the synchronized folder and accessible on the Raspberry Pi in read-only mode.

item.title: Traefik behind AWS ELB. X-Forwarded-For header
item.description:
Traefik v1.7 is running within a Docker Swarm, functioning as a global service. Consequently, an instance of Traefik is deployed for each host within our Docker Swarm cluster. It is important to note that the Swarm’s routing mesh is not employed in this setup. Instead, the port mode is configured as “host,” allowing the port to be attached to the host’s network.

traefik:
  image: ${REGISTRY}/traefik:1.6
  build: ./traefik
  volumes:
    - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
  networks:
    - traefik
  environment:
    - AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=${AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID}
    - AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=${AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY}
    - AWS_REGION=${AWS_REGION}
    - AWS_HOSTED_ZONE_ID=${AWS_HOSTED_ZONE_ID}
  ports:
    - target: 80
      published: 80
      protocol: tcp
      mode: host
    - target: 443
      published: 443
      mode: host
      protocol: tcp
    - target: 8080
      published: 8080
      mode: host
      protocol: tcp
  deploy:
    mode: global
    restart_policy:
      condition: on-failure

Docker Swarm deployed to AWS and lives behind internal/external load balancers (Classic type).

item.title: Custom error page in Traefik v2.x. Finally
item.description:
What you will learn today 🦦:

  • what is Traefik and why I decisively recommend to use it with Docker Swarm
  • how to show custom error page if Traefik returns 404 (service not found)

How I met Traefik 😁

Traefik is a top modern reverse proxy and load balancer. I started to use it from major version 1. At that moment, I already had Docker Swarm cluster, which I began to prepare for production use, and only one thing in the coherent picture has been left: “External Load Balancer”. Official docker doc had an example with HaProxy so it was obvious to try it out first. As I remember it took a significant amount of time to get HaProxy works smoothly with Swarm. But I wasn’t satisfied with the result:

item.title: Solution for CodeSignal: addTwoHugeNumbers (Linked List part 👯)
item.description:
Task description on Codesignal

I’ve spend decent amount of time in order to figure out that I don’t need any string manipulation in this task 😅. My first try was adding missing zeros to the beginning of number slot, convert int to str and back, then the resulting string split by 4 but no luck … It turned out that is simple math 😀 I wanna share my solution with detailed comments ⬇️ (Time complexity O(n) )

item.title: XDebug for PHP docker container on macOS
